flashcards.front: What is the primary function of mass storage devices in a computer system?
flashcards.back: Mass storage devices provide persistent, non-volatile, high-capacity storage for data and programs that must be retained when the computer is powered off.

flashcards.front: Define 'seek time' in magnetic disk operations.
flashcards.back: Seek time is the time taken by the disk arm to move the read/write head to the correct track. It is typically measured in milliseconds and is a major component of disk access time.

flashcards.front: What is rotational latency in hard disk drives?
flashcards.back: Rotational latency is the time required for the desired sector to rotate under the read/write head. It depends on the rotational speed of the disk (typically 5400-15000 RPM).

flashcards.front: Explain the term 'track' in magnetic disk terminology.
flashcards.back: A track is a concentric circle on the surface of a disk platter where data is stored. Each track is divided into sectors, and multiple tracks form the circular paths on the disk.

flashcards.front: What is a cylinder in disk geometry?
flashcards.back: A cylinder is the set of all tracks with the same radial distance from the center on all platters. When the disk arm is at a particular position, all heads can read corresponding tracks forming a cylinder.

flashcards.front: Define 'transfer rate' for disk I/O operations.
flashcards.back: Transfer rate is the speed at which data is transferred between the disk and the computer's memory. It is measured in MB/s and depends on factors like rotational speed and sector size.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of disk scheduling algorithms?
flashcards.back: Disk scheduling algorithms minimize seek time and rotational latency by determining the optimal order in which to service pending I/O requests, improving overall system performance.
FCFS services disk requests in the order they arrive in the request queue. It is simple but does not optimize for seek time, potentially causing poor performance.

flashcards.front: How does the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm work?
flashcards.back: SCAN (also called elevator algorithm) moves the disk arm from one end to the other, servicing all requests along the way, then reverses direction. It reduces average seek time compared to FCFS.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between SCAN and C-SCAN scheduling?
flashcards.back: C-SCAN (Circular SCAN) returns to the beginning without servicing requests during the return trip, giving all requests equal wait time. SCAN services requests in both directions.

flashcards.front: What is wear leveling in SSDs?
flashcards.back: Wear leveling is a technique used in SSDs to distribute write operations evenly across all memory cells, preventing premature wear of specific blocks and extending the SSD's lifespan.

flashcards.front: Explain garbage collection in Solid State Drives.
flashcards.back: Garbage collection is the process of erasing valid data from flash memory blocks and making previously written but now unused blocks available for new writes.

flashcards.front: What is a block in flash memory terminology?
flashcards.back: A block is the smallest erasable unit in NAND flash memory. Blocks typically contain 64-256 pages and can only be erased as a whole, not individually.

flashcards.front: Define 'page' in the context of NAND flash memory.
flashcards.back: A page is the smallest programmable unit in NAND flash memory, typically 2KB-16KB in size. Pages can be written individually but cannot be erased individually.

flashcards.front: What is RAID 0 in storage technology?
flashcards.back: RAID 0 (striping) distributes data across multiple disks without redundancy. It improves performance by allowing parallel read/write operations but provides no fault tolerance.
